coredata.dc:title: On-site colorimetric food spoilage monitoring with smartphone embedded machine learning
coredata.dc:creator: Doğan, Vakkas
Evliya, Melodi
Nesrin Kahyaoglu, Leyla
Kılıç, Volkan
coredata.dc:description: Real-time and on-site food spoilage monitoring is still a challenging issue to prevent food poisoning. At the onset of food spoilage, microbial and enzymatic activities lead to the formation of volatile amines. Monitoring of these amines with conventional methods requires sophisticated, costly, labor-intensive, and time consuming analysis. Here, anthocyanins rich red cabbage extract (ARCE) based colorimetric sensing system was developed with the incorporation of embedded machine learning in a smartphone application for real-time food spoilage monitoring. FG-UV-CD100 films were first fabricated by crosslinking ARCE-doped fish gelatin (FG) with carbon dots (CDs) under UV light. The color change of FG-UV-CD100 films with varying ammonia vapor concentrations was captured in different light sources with smartphones of various brands, and a comprehensive dataset was created to train machine learning (ML) classifiers to be robust and adaptable to ambient conditions, resulting in 98.8% classification accuracy. Meanwhile, the ML classifier was embedded into our Android application, SmartFood++, enabling analysis in about 0.1 s without internet access, unlike its counterpart using cloud operation via internet. The proposed system was also tested on a real fish sample with 99.6% accuracy, demonstrating that it has a great advantage as a potent tool for on-site real-time monitoring of food spoilage by non-specialized personnel.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Colorimetric detection
Machine learning
Smartphone application
Fish gelatin
Spoilage monitoring

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the Scientific and Technological Research Council of Turkey (TUBITAK) (No. 119O864 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the proposed system.
The general structure of Random Forest.
Confusion matrix of RF in different concentrations.

The changes in TVB-N and TVC contents of fish samples during 8 days of storage at 4 °C. Inset images indicate the color response of the films for each storage day. Different letters on each symbol represent significant differences within given groups (p < 0.05).

Screenshots of the SmartFood++ for spoilage monitoring. Gallery, camera and crop icons are located at the bottom of the homepage as shown in (a). The image can be uploaded either from the gallery as in (b), or by capturing with the camera. Then, the ROI in the image is selected as demonstrated in (c), (d), (g) and (h) to crop the patches in (e) and (i) for the different test samples. The quantitative and qualitative evaluation results are given in (f) and (j).
Optical and camera characteristics of smartphones.
The color change of FG-UV-CD100 film with varying concentrations of ammonia vapor.
Performance results of RF for colorimetric food spoilage monitoring.

head.ce:title: On-site colorimetric food spoilage monitoring with smartphone embedded machine learning

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical and Electronics Engineering, Izmir Katip Celebi University, 35620 Izmir, Turkey

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Electrical and Electronics Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Electrical and Electronics Engineering, Izmir Katip Celebi University, 35620 Izmir, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food Engineering, Middle East Technical University, 06800 Ankara, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food Engineering, Middle East Technical University, 06800 Ankara, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Real-time and on-site food spoilage monitoring is still a challenging issue to prevent food poisoning. At the onset of food spoilage, microbial and enzymatic activities lead to the formation of volatile amines. Monitoring of these amines with conventional methods requires sophisticated, costly, labor-intensive, and time consuming analysis. Here, anthocyanins rich red cabbage extract (ARCE) based colorimetric sensing system was developed with the incorporation of embedded machine learning in a smartphone application for real-time food spoilage monitoring. FG-UV-CD100 films were first fabricated by crosslinking ARCE-doped fish gelatin (FG) with carbon dots (CDs) under UV light. The color change of FG-UV-CD100 films with varying ammonia vapor concentrations was captured in different light sources with smartphones of various brands, and a comprehensive dataset was created to train machine learning (ML) classifiers to be robust and adaptable to ambient conditions, resulting in 98.8% classification accuracy. Meanwhile, the ML classifier was embedded into our Android application, SmartFood++, enabling analysis in about 0.1 s without internet access, unlike its counterpart using cloud operation via internet. The proposed system was also tested on a real fish sample with 99.6% accuracy, demonstrating that it has a great advantage as a potent tool for on-site real-time monitoring of food spoilage by non-specialized personnel.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Food waste is a globally growing concern due to its potential impacts on ecological, social and economic consequences, including greenhouse gas emissions, nutritional insecurity, unsustainable production and distribution chain [1]. According to the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations, annual food waste reaches 1.3 billion tonnes [2]. On the other hand, the world population is expected to increase by almost 25% and reach nearly 10 billion by 2050 [3]. This drastic population growth means increased consumption of highly perishable protein-rich food, and thereby an expected increase in food waste [4]. Concern about how to ensure the sustainability and safety of food production for the rapidly growing world population has led to the development of new strategies, including innovative packaging applications.

Smart packaging systems offer sustainable approaches to reduce food waste by incorporating active and intelligent compounds into biodegradable and biocompatible polymers [5]. Normally, conventional packaging aims only to maintain food safety and quality by serving as a physical barrier, while smart packaging presents beyond the role of the physical barrier by adding specific functionality to conventional packaging [6]. Thanks to their antioxidant and halochromic properties, anthocyanins, natural color pigments, have been widely used in smart packaging applications [7].

Recent studies have demonstrated the advantage of anthocyanins as colorimetric freshness indicators in intelligent food packaging labels [8–10]. The freshness of food is often monitored by manual observation of the color change of anthocyanins, which is time-consuming and sensitive to human perception [11–13]. Therefore, there is a need for an automated system that can detect a color change to assess the freshness of the food to improve monitoring performance.

The color analysis is mainly based on the intensity values in various color spaces consisting of three or four channels such as RGB (Red, Green, Blue), YUV (Luminance, Blue-Luminance, Red-Luminance), L*a*b* (Lightness, Green-Red, Blue-Yellow), CMYK (Cyan, Magenta, Yellow, Key) and HSV (Hue, Saturation, Value) which have been employed in many applications including analyte detection [14] and freshness monitoring [10]. The conventional approach is to use single or multiple channels to derive the calibration curve that establishes a relationship between intensity and output in the colorimetric analysis [15]. The calibration curve offers simplicity and ease of use, making it widely used for colorimetric analysis in a controlled environment [16]. However, intensity values are easily affected by the camera optics, brightness, and source of the lights, causing the curve to be recalibrated for specific conditions [17].

To overcome this problem and increase robustness, advanced algorithms such as machine learning (ML) have been used, which learn how to classify inputs based on features extracted from color information. In [18], a ML-based freshness detection was developed for food using colorimetric analysis obtained from a glycerol-based sensory film. A colorimetric sensor was used to detect H2O2 with ML classifiers for air sampling [19]. The alcohol level in saliva was determined using three ML classifiers with features extracted from four color channels [20]. In addition to its robustness, ML is highly preferred due to its adaptability and easy integration into smartphone-based imaging systems for quantitative and qualitative colorimetric analysis. SPAQ [21] and SPAQ2 [20] applications were developed to detect the alcohol level in saliva. GlucoSensing [22] application was developed to determine glucose concentration. A low-cost paper-based microfluidic device for nitrite concentration measurement and pH determination was integrated with an Android application [23]. ChemTrainer [24] application used cloud systems to transfer the images from a smartphone to a remote server for processing with ML classifiers. GlucoQuantifier also set a link to the remote server via cloud systems for glucose quantification [25]. One drawback of the mentioned studies is the necessity of cloud operations to transfer the data to the remote server for colorimetric analysis with ML classifiers. Internet access is required due to cloud operations, causing inoperable analysis in resource-limited environments. The speed of the internet is also critical to complete the data transfer, which affects the response time for analysis. Moreover, remote servers need to be kept up and running all the time, resulting in higher operating and maintenance costs.

In our previous study, FG-UV-CD100 film demonstrated the best physicochemical and colorimetric properties in the freshness monitoring of chicken samples [10]. In this study, ML was embedded in a smartphone application for colorimetric analysis of fish freshness with our previously developed and characterized FG-UV-CD100 films. The color response of FG-UV-CD100 against nine different volatile ammonia concentrations was collected using smartphone cameras to enable concentration detection with an ML classifier. In that sense, the classifiers were trained with a dataset including images for color variation of FG-UV-CD100 film with respect to the ammonia concentration. To ensure robustness against camera optics and diverse illuminations, the images were captured with four smartphones in three exposure angles under seven illuminations. The Random Forest (RF), which had the highest accuracy among all classifiers, was embedded into our custom-designed Android application SmartFood++. Unlike other Android applications that require internet access for data transfer to the ML classifier in the remoter server, SmartFood++ includes an embedded ML classifier that can complete analysis in around 0.1 s. To the best of our knowledge, an ML classifier was embedded into a smartphone application and linked with fish gelatin (FG) films for the first time, enabling rapid and portable colorimetric food spoilage monitoring on-site surveillance. The proposed system (Fig. 1 ) was also tested in a real sample (e.g., fish) in which the results proved its advantages and potential in food spoilage monitoring.

Fish gelatin was purchased from SG Chemicals. Magnesium oxide, boric acid, sodium monobasic, potassium dibasic phosphates, and methyl red-bromocresol green solution were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich. Fuming hydrochloric acid (37%), ammonium chloride, and citric acid monohydrate were acquired from ISOLAB Merck supplied ammonium hydroxide solution (32%).

The method used in this study was adapted from methods described in [10]. First, anthocyanins-rich red cabbage extract (ARCE) was prepared following the method [26] where chopped red cabbage was put in a beaker with distilled water at a 1:2 cabbage: water (w/v) ratio and left stirring overnight. The ARCE was then filtered and stored at 4 °C in a dark environment until further use. The concentration of the ARCE was determined with a modified pH differential method [27]. Next, carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized based on microwave-assisted carbonization of citric acid [28]. In brief, CDs were synthesized from a citric acid monohydrate solution of 10% w/v in distilled water by microwave-assisted pyrolysis method at 550 W for 10 min in a conventional microwave oven [28]. Next, the synthesized CDs were freeze-dried and used to prepare a 100 mg/mL stock solution. FG-UV-CD100 films were prepared via the solvent casting method. FG, ARCE, glycerol, and CDs were mixed and ultrasonicated for 90 min at a final concentration of 10% w/v, 0.5 mg/L, 1% w/v, and 100 mg/L, respectively. 20 mL of the precursor solution was placed in a square Petri dish with a size of 10 cm × 10 cm and treated with 365 nm UV light for 45 min (1.5 mW/cm). Next, petri dishes with the precursor solution were put in a desiccator (0% RH) at 23 °C for 48 h. Before further use, FG-UV-CD100 films were placed inside a climate chamber with 50% RH at 20 °C and conditioned for at least 24 h before further use.
Here, ML processes, including feature extraction and selection, and classification with RF, are introduced for colorimetric analysis of food spoilage monitoring.

Feature extraction, a crucial step in ML training, is a size reduction process through the efficient representation of an image with information such as color, texture, size, shape, and location [31]. Removing redundant or irrelevant information from the image increases the accuracy of the classifiers as the learning relies on relevant features and reduced dimensionality. In that sense, the number and type of the features are critical for the image representation and thus directly linked to the classifier performance. Here, features are extracted based on color information, which offers adequate representation for images. The film response, namely region of interest (ROI), was converted from RGB images to YUV, L*a*b*, CMYK and HSV, to obtain the individual channels (R, G, B, Y, U, V, L*, a*, b*, C, M, Y, K, H, S, and V) for color feature extraction. In addition, statistical calculations, such as mean, standard deviation and kurtosis, were also used to expand the dataset for each channel, leading to forty-eight features. To further reduce the computational complexity and improve the performance, feature selection was employed as discussed next.

The identification process of the most relevant subset features is called as feature selection, providing robust and improved classification with reduced computational burden [32]. Eliminating irrelevant features contributes to computational efficiency, leading to a rapid response in colorimetric analysis [33]. In that sense, various feature selection algorithms were employed including ReliefF [34], Mutual Information [35], Gain Ratio [36], Information Gain [37], Fisher's [38], Correlation Coefficient [39], and Chi-Squared [40]. These algorithms were used to select the most relevant subset among the forty-eight features. To find the best feature selection algorithm, they were tested with the random forest to create benchmarks showing their contribution to classification accuracy. The results in Table S2 showed that the Chi-Squared algorithm outperformed the others in terms of classification accuracy.

In the Chi-Squared (χ2) algorithm, a t
i feature set is selected based on its correlation with a C
j class, and the discrimination ability (χ2) of the t
i feature set with respect to the C
j class is calculated as:
(1)
χ 2
(
t i
,
C j
)
=
S ×
a
i j
×
d
i j
-
b
i j
×
c
i j
2
a
i j
×
b
i j
+
c
i j
×
c
i j
×
b
i j
+
d
i j
where S is the total number of samples, the
a
i j
is the number of instances in the category
C j
containing the attribute
t i
, and the
b
i j
is the number of instances in the category
C j
that does not contain the attribute
t i
. The
c
i j
is the number of samples that contain the
t i
attribute; however, it does not belong to the
C j
category. The
d
i j
is the number of samples that do not belong to the
C j
category and do not contain the
t i
attribute.

After the Chi-Squared feature selection, eighteen irrelevant features were eliminated and the number of features was narrowed down to thirty (Fig. S1). Then, the ML classifiers were trained again with a new feature set, improving the classification accuracy of RF from 97.49% to 98.80%.

To monitor food spoilage based on color variation in film-response images, twenty-three ML classifiers were tested using the finalized feature set. After comparison in terms of accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, ROC Area, and Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC), the RF was chosen due to its superior performance compared to its counterparts.

The RF consists of multiple decision trees and nodes to utilize ensemble learning which combines many classifiers to solve classification and regression problems [41]. In the RF, the best subset of randomly selected estimators is used to split each node, whereas, in other standard trees, each node is split using the best distribution among all variables. Therefore, the RF is more robust against overfitting, an undesirable ML problem. It uses only two variables, including variable and tree numbers in the random subset and forest, respectively [42]. A structure of the RF classifier is shown in Fig. 2 .

SmartFood++, our ML-based Android application for food spoilage monitoring, was improved to perform colorimetric analysis without a remote server and an internet connection (offline). In the SmartFood++ application, the classification was run in Android with an embedded RF, resulting in more robustness than SmartFood [10], which was the previous version running with the calibration curve. In addition, the interface of the application has been improved to be simple and user-friendly with designs and icons.

To run an embedded ML classifier in the application, both the feature extraction and the ML model need to be Java-compatible as the application was developed in Android Studio with Java language. To test an ML classifier in the application, the input must be represented with the same feature types used in training the classifier. Therefore, the feature extraction script was first coded in the Eclipse IDE for Java Developers (2022–06) before being integrated into the application in Android Studio. The same script was also used to create a “.CSV” file, containing the feature set to train the RF classifier. To ensure the compatibility of the trained model with Android, the Weka 3.9.6 environment was used as it was also developed in Java. The CSV file was then converted to ARFF (“.arff”) file extension for the training of the RF in the Weka. After the training, it was saved with the model extension file (“.model”) to embed into the Android application.

With the improved interface, even non-expert users can easily perform operations such as uploading, viewing and cropping images. The image can be uploaded to the application in two ways: an image can be selected by the user from the gallery as in Fig. 5b, or a new image can be captured using the built-in camera. Then, using the adjustable crop box, the ROI can be determined from the image as in Fig. 5c, d, g and h. When the “CLASSIFY” button is tapped in Fig. 5e and i, the concentration value can be classified based on the RF classifier and displayed on the screen (Fig. 5f and j).

Freshly caught horse mackerels were purchased from a local market (Ankara, Turkey) and transported immediately to the laboratory in an ice bucket. Horse mackerel samples (25–30 g) were put into square petri plates (10 cm × 10 cm). A film piece of 1 cm × 1 cm was attached to the cellophane and then placed 1 cm above the fish sample. The plates were kept at 4 °C during 8 days of storage. The color of the films was monitored daily by taking images of the films with a smartphone.

The fish sample (10 g) was placed in a stomacher bag with 100 mL sterile DI water and homogenized for 5 min. Next, the supernatant was removed from the solids after centrifugation (10 min at 15,000 rpm) and added to an equal volume of 1% w/v magnesium oxide solution. The mixture was steam distilled into a flask containing 25 mL of 20 g/L boric acid solution. After the distillation, a few drops of methyl red/bromocresol green indicator were added to the distillate and 0.01 M HCl was used to titrate the solution until the blue color became pink. The TVB-N level of the samples was reported as mg N/100 g using the weight of the sample and the volume of HCl solution used during titration for the sample and blank [43].

10 g of fish sample was added to 90 mL of sterile peptone water, and the resulting mixture was homogenized in a stomacher for 2 min. Several decimal dilutions were prepared in series. 1 mL of each dilution was spread on plate count agar. Inoculated plates were inverted and incubated at 37 °C for 48 h. The unit for the total viable count (TVC) was reported as colony-forming units per gram (cfu/g).

Origin Pro 2021 (OriginLab Co., Wellesley, MA, USA) was used to determine mean differences by analysis of variance (ANOVA) with Tukey's multiple comparison tests. Significant differences were defined for p ≤ 0.05.

Gases including ammonia, trimethylamine, and dimethylamine, also referred to as TVB-N compounds, are formed when protein-rich foods degrade as a result of microbial and enzymatic activity. Ammonia, which among these volatile nitrogenous compounds, has the lowest boiling point and is released rapidly at the onset of spoilage of fish and meat products and thereby can be used as a model TVB-N compound to develop food freshness labels [44]. In response to ammonia and other TVB-N compounds, visible color changes of anthocyanins are expected as a result of their structural conversion. Therefore, we collected images of the developed films treated with different concentrations of ammonia. As illustrated in Table 2, the color of the films displayed a drastic change from bright red to dark green when films were exposed to ammonia concentration above 20 mg N/100 g. The production of ammonium ions by the hydration and hydrolysis of ammonia vapor results in an alkaline environment on the surface of the films [45]. Under basic conditions, deprotonation on the phenolic hydroxyl groups of anthocyanins leads to color change [46]. Altogether, these results indicated that ARCE-loaded films were sensitive enough to detect changes in fish freshness.

Here, we report ML-based colorimetric monitoring of food spoilage using the color change in FG-UV-CD100 films resulting from the interaction of TVB-N compounds with anthocyanins. To ensure the robustness of the classifier, a comprehensive dataset was created using color variation in FG-UV-CD100 films to nine different ammonia gas concentrations with four smartphones. The images were captured under seven different illumination and three exposure angles. Then, they were processed to extract forty-eight features from the ROI on films. Next, the Chi-Squared algorithm was used for feature selection to obtain a new subset with thirty features. These features were used to train twenty-three ML classifiers and RF outperformed other classifiers as shown in Table S1. The classifiers were compared with respect to recall-TP Rate, FP Rate, classification accuracy, precision, and F1-score formulated as;
(2)
T P
R a t e = R e c a l l =
T P
T P + F N
(3)
F P
R a t e =
F P
F P + T N
(4)
A c c u r a c y =
T P + T N
T P + T N + F P + F N
(5)
P r e c i s i o n =
T P
T P + F P
(6)
F 1
s c o r e = 2
x
P r e c i s i o n
x
R e c a l l
P r e c i s i o n + R e c a l l
where TP is True-Positive, which refers to a sample of the positive class in both ground truth and classification. If the sample belongs to the negative class and is classified as a negative, it is called True-Negative (TN). If a sample from a negative class is classified as a positive, it is called a False-Positive (FP). False-Negative (FN) is the case when a sample from a positive class is classified as a negative. Precision is the ratio of the TP to all positives. The recall is the metric used to measure classification precision as the ratio of TP to the sum of TP and FN. Lastly, the F1-score is the weighted harmonic mean of precision and recall.

The ROC area is another method used to determine the performance of models in classification, calculated by
(7)
R O C
A r e a =
1 2
x
(
T P
T P + F N
+
T N
T N + F P
)
.

The MCC is also employed to complete analysis across multiple performance metrics, calculated as
(8)
M C C =
(
T P
x
T N
)
−
(
F P
x
F N
)
(
T P + T N
)
(
T P
x
F N
)
(
F P
x
T N
)
(
F N
x
T N
)
.

The performance metrics results of ML classifiers in Table S1 demonstrated that RF outperformed other classifiers for colorimetric food spoilage detection. The ROC area under these curves (AUC) has also proved that the RF has successfully classified the concentrations (Figs. S2–10). Classification metric results and confusion matrix are available in Table 3 and Fig. 3 a and b, respectively. The correlation between the true and predicted classes of the RF classifier was visualized with confusion matrices. In Table 3, the metric results are below the average values for 40 and 50 mg N/100 g concentrations which can be seen in the confusion matrix (Fig. 3a and b) where the true and predicted classes of RF for each concentration value of FG-UV-CD100 films are illustrated. Next, the RF classifier was embedded into our smartphone application, SmartFood++, for colorimetric food spoilage detection.

In addition, the proposed system was tested on a real sample to demonstrate its robustness and performance. In that sense, the fish (horse mackerel) sample was monitored and captured with smartphone cameras under seven illumination conditions to create a dataset specific to fish spoilage. After feature extraction and selection were employed, the RF was trained with a fish-feature set and showed 99.6% test accuracy. The demonstration with an embedded RF classifier to SmartFood++ was given in Fig. 5, where the two different selected images were processed and classified in the application after cropping with the adjustable crop box (Fig. 5c-d-g-h). Then, the SmartFood++ correctly classified the fish samples as 10 mg N/100 g and 120 mg N/100 g, as shown in Fig. 5f and j. The comparison results in Fig. 5f and j proved that processing times (149 ms–68 ms) were significantly reduced, providing real-time colorimetric analysis for fish samples. In addition, SmartFood++ informs the user based on the test result with two types of stickers, including happy-face and no-food. As in Fig. 5f, the text “Bon Appetit” is displayed on the screen under the happy-face sticker since the fish (food) is consumable. Otherwise, as in Fig. 5j, the text “Inconsumable” is shown with a no-food sticker.

Finally, in our study, to evaluate how the colorimetric label responded to fish spoiling in the package, the color changes of the FG-UV-CD100 films were monitored during storage using a smartphone. Following death, the quantity of microorganisms on the skin and gill surfaces steadily rises and disperses throughout numerous tissues. The microorganisms in question are commonly Pseudomonas spp. and are often called spoiling microorganisms [47]. As a result of these microorganisms, volatile organic compounds, collectively called TVB-N compounds, are formed. Thus, the level of TVB-N compounds is a good indicator of fish microbial quality and freshness along with TVC [27]. During storage, these volatile compounds were expected to interact with the amine-sensitive anthocyanins within the packaging film matrix and cause a color change from first red, then blue, and finally green. In several studies, TVB-N and TVC levels were monitored and used to predict the freshness of fish samples [27,48–50] while the corresponding color changes of intelligent labels were used to determine the degree of freshness. Correspondingly, decaying fish resulted in a color change of the film from red to dark green, while the fish samples’ TVB-N and, subsequently, TVC levels considerably increased during storage at 4 °C (Fig. 4
). The initial TVB-N content and TVC level of fish were 8.54 mg N/100 g and 4.21 log cfu/g, respectively. For the first 4 days of the storage, there was no notable increase in the TVB-N and TVC values. After this period, the deterioration rate increased, as evidenced by the rapidly growing values of TVB-N and TVC. As stated in Turkish Codex Regulation 2012/73 on sensory properties of fishery products and volatile total base nitrogen limits, the acceptable upper limit of TVB-N is set at 25 mg N/100 g while the TVC limit is 7 log cfu/g for fish, in general [51]. The TVB-N and TVC content reached 30.80 mg N/100 g and 6.68 log cfu/g on day 5, respectively, indicating the onset of food spoilage. The limit of detection (LOD) of the sensor was calculated to be 7.31 mg N/100 g based on color intensity (YUV data) of images (LOD = 3.3 × σ/Slope). Here, machine learning and colorimetric analysis are integrated for the first time under an Android application for food spoilage. The similar studies [20, 52, 53, 54] reported machine learning based colorimetric analysis. However, the proposed study differentiates itself from them by embedding the ML model into Android applications that reduces the response time to about 0.01 s. Unlike existing studies that require more than minutes for colorimetric analysis due to internet-based cloud and server operations, this study does not require online connections, which reduces the cost and enables real-time video processing. Multiple frames from the video can provide more information than a single image, resulting in improved food monitoring.

Herein, we demonstrated a new implementation of ML classifiers that has the potential for on-site real-time monitoring of food spoilage by incorporating FG films with embedded RF into SmartFood++ application. These FG films have the ability to change color in response to the accumulation of ammonia released from spoiled food. The colorimetric response was captured in seven different illumination conditions and three exposure angles using four smartphones to create a comprehensive dataset for the training ML classifiers, leading to improved robustness against variation in illumination and camera optics. The highest classification accuracy (98.8%) was achieved with RF, proving that the proposed system has great potential in colorimetric food spoilage monitoring. The system was also trained and tested with real fish samples, resulting in 99.6% accuracy. In addition, the RF classifier was embedded in SmartFood++, allowing analysis in about 0.1 s without internet access. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study that embeds an ML classifier into a smartphone application for food spoilage monitoring. Unlike conventional measurements, our system offers real-time, robust and easy-operation for non-expert users which can lead new tools with advanced functions for smart packaging.

Vakkas Doğan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Writing-Original draft preparation. Melodi Evliya: Data curation, Investigation, Writing-Original draft preparation. Leyla Nesrin Kahyaoglu: Supervision, Writing-Reviewing and Editing. Volkan Kılıç: Supervision, Writing-Reviewing and Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Scientific and Technological Research Council of Turkey (TUBITAK) (No. 119O864).

The following is the Supplementary data to this article.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2023.125021.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The effects of reducing food losses and food waste on global food insecurity, natural resources, and greenhouse gas emissions
Environ. Econ. Pol. Stud.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Munesue, T. Masui, T. Fushima, The effects of reducing food losses and food waste on global food insecurity, natural resources, and green- house gas emissions, Environmental Economics and Policy Studies 17 (2015) 43-77.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances on intelligent packaging as tools to reduce food waste
J. Clean. Prod.
